questions.question: Define the following in First-Order Logic: (a) Term (b) Atomic Formula (c) Well-Formed Formula (WFF)
keyPoints: Term: constant, variable, or function applied to terms
Atomic formula: predicate symbol applied to terms
WFF: recursively defined - atoms are WFFs; connectives and quantifiers preserve WFF status

questions.question: Explain the difference between free variables and bound variables with an example.
keyPoints: Free variable: not bound by any quantifier in the formula
Bound variable: falls within scope of a quantifier
Example: In ∃x (P(x) ∧ Q(y)), x is bound, y is free
Free variables require assignments to determine truth; bound variables are quantified away

questions.question: What is an interpretation in FOL? Explain the components of an interpretation.
keyPoints: Interpretation assigns meaning to non-logical symbols
Components: Domain (non-empty set D), Constant assignment (Ic), Function assignment (If), Predicate assignment (Ip)
I = (D, Ic, If, Ip)
Interpretation provides semantics for evaluating truth values

questions.question: Explain Universal and Existential quantifiers with examples.
keyPoints: Universal (∀x): 'for all x' - asserts predicate holds for every element in domain
Existential (∃x): 'there exists x' - asserts predicate holds for at least one element
Example: Domain = {1,2,3}; ∀x Even(x) is false; ∃x Even(x) is true (2 is even)
Negation: ¬∀x = ∃x¬ ; ¬∃x = ∀x¬ (De Morgan's laws)

questions.question: What is a sentence (closed formula) in FOL? How does it differ from an open formula?
keyPoints: Sentence: formula with no free variables - all variables are bound
Open formula: contains at least one free variable
Example: P(x) is open (x is free); ∀x P(x) is closed/sentence
Sentences have definite truth values under interpretations; open formulas need variable assignments

questions.question: Differentiate between syntax and semantics of First-Order Logic.
keyPoints: Syntax: formal rules for constructing valid formulas (alphabet, terms, formation rules)
Semantics: meaning assigned to symbols (interpretations, truth values)
Syntax is concerned with structure/validity; semantics with truth/meaning
Same syntax can have different semantics under different interpretations

questions.question: Explain the following with examples: (a) Ground Term (b) Scope of a Quantifier (c) Model of a formula
keyPoints: Ground Term: term with no variables (e.g., f(a,b) where a,b constants)
Scope of Quantifier: the subformula following the quantifier
Model: interpretation that makes formula true (I ⊨ φ if φ is true in I)
Example: In ∀x P(x), scope is P(x); if domain={1} and P(1)=true, then interpretation is model

questions.question: Explain in detail the syntax and semantics of First-Order Logic with suitable examples.
keyPoints: Syntax: Alphabet (variables, constants, function/predicate symbols, connectives, quantifiers); Terms (constants, variables, functions); Atomic formulas (predicates on terms); WFF formation rules (recursion)
Semantics: Interpretation I=(D,Ic,If,Ip); Variable assignment; Truth evaluation rules for connectives, quantifiers
Example showing evaluation: Domain={1,2}, P(x)=x>1, evaluate ∀x P(x), ∃x P(x)
Distinction between syntax (form) and semantics (meaning); multiple interpretations possible
Show how same formula gets different truth values under different interpretations

questions.question: Explain the process of determining whether a First-Order Logic formula is true or false under a given interpretation with a detailed example.
keyPoints: Step 1: Identify interpretation components - domain, constant/function/predicate assignments
Step 2: For formulas with free variables, need variable assignment
Step 3: Evaluate atomic formulas using predicate assignments
Step 4: Evaluate compound formulas using truth tables for connectives
Step 5: For quantified formulas, check predicate for each element in domain
Detailed example: Evaluate ∃x (P(x) ∧ Q(x)) for domain D={a,b}, P(a)=true, P(b)=false, Q(a)=true, Q(b)=true
Result: Check x=a: P(a)∧Q(a)=true∧true=true; so formula is true
Explanation of model concept: if formula is true under interpretation, interpretation is model

questions.question: Discuss quantifiers in FOL. Explain how the order of quantifiers affects the meaning of formulas, with examples.
keyPoints: Two quantifiers: ∀ (universal) and ∃ (existential)
Quantifier binding: binds variable in its scope
Order matters: ∀x∃y P(x,y) ≠ ∃y∀x P(x,y)
Example 1: ∀x∃y Loves(x,y) - everyone loves someone (possibly different)
Example 2: ∃y∀x Loves(x,y) - there exists someone loved by everyone
Proof of difference with counterexample domain {a,b}
De Morgan's laws: ¬∀x = ∃x¬ , ¬∃x = ∀x¬
Nested quantifiers require careful analysis of scope and order

questions.question: What is an interpretation and a model in First-Order Logic? Explain with an example how you determine if an interpretation is a model for a formula.
keyPoints: Interpretation I assigns meaning: Domain D (non-empty set); Constant symbols→elements of D; Function symbols→functions; Predicate symbols→relations
Model: An interpretation that makes a formula true (I ⊨ φ)
Example: Formula: ∀x (P(x) → Q(x))
Interpretation: D={1,2}, P(1)=true, P(2)=false, Q(1)=false, Q(2)=true
Evaluation: For x=1: P(1)→Q(1)=true→false=false; For x=2: P(2)→Q(2)=false→true=true
Since for all x, implication is true, interpretation is a model
If any assignment makes formula false, interpretation is NOT a model
Discussion of valid formulas (true in all interpretations) and satisfiable formulas (true in some interpretation)
